sop.coo_evaluation:
# SOP Audit: The Forge Belfast

**Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Complaint & incident handling** – no documented protocol for member complaints, injuries, safeguarding concerns, or disputes (critical for liability & retention)
- **Financial controls & reconciliation** – no SOP for cash handling, refunds, chargeback disputes, or monthly P&L review
- **Member onboarding beyond first class** – no structured 30/60/90-day check-in or progression pathway
- **Win-back/reactivation campaigns** – no process for reaching lapsed members or win-back sequences
- **Staff disciplinary & performance management** – no SOP for underperformance, coaching quality issues, or termination
- **Health screening & medical clearance** – no documented intake or flags for high-risk members (PAR-Q follow-up, contraindications)
- **Retention metrics & churn analysis** – no monthly review process to identify and act on drop-off signals
- **Emergency response & first aid** – no SOP for medical emergencies, AED use, or accident reporting
- **Scheduling conflicts & class cancellations** – no clear protocol for managing full classes, waitlists, or last-minute cancellations

**Needs updating**

- **Coaches 17/17** – likely missing: quality assurance audits, form checks on new members, handling difficult/toxic members, de-escalation tactics
- **Clients 22/22** – review for: retention milestones, membership upgrade prompts, payment failure handling, feedback loops post-class
- **Marketing 10/12** – the two missing (Newsletter, Blog) are symptomatic; audit existing 10 for brand consistency, audience segmentation, lead-to-member conversion tracking
- **Facilities 4/4** – audit for: deep-clean schedules, equipment

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO BRIEF

## INCREASE REVENUE

- **Pricing is your biggest lever.** June revenue dropped 12% month-on-month (£20,199 → £17,711) despite adding 25 net members (122 → 147). Your AR/M collapsed from £165.56 to £120.48 — that's a 27% drop. You're acquiring members but at lower price points or with heavy discounting. Immediately audit your June intake: were discounts offered? Run a pricing test — raise core membership by £5–8/month on new signups this month. With 147 members at even £10 extra each, that's £1,470/month incremental profit.
- **Retention cliff in April/May signals onboarding failure post-relocation.** Churn spiked 8.33% (April) and 5.74% (May) as you moved to a bigger studio — members didn't feel the value of the new space fast enough. June recovered to 0% churn, so the message landed. Codify what worked in June and lock it into standard member welcome: studio tour, challenge card, group photo, first class feedback call. Target: hold churn below 3%.
- **Fill the quiet hours with upsells, not discounts.** You've doubled studio size; quiet slots are revenue dead-weight. Introduce a "peak-time premium" (£2–3 extra per session for 6–8am and 5–7pm slots), and offer "off-peak discounts" (£1 off) only for pre-booked 11am or 2pm classes. This inverts the discount and crowds your margin-dense hours.
- **New revenue line: PT/small group training.** At 147 members you have 20–25 candidates for 1:1 or semi-private (£35–50/session). You've got the space now. Hire or partner with 1 PT at £20/hour; run 10 sessions/week = £300–500/week incremental revenue, minimal overhead.

## REDUCE EXPENSES

- **Non-people expenses are the problem.** May–June non-people costs spiked: £9,233 (May) → £9,390 (June). That's 46–53% of revenue, vs. 31% in December. Drill into this line immediately: is it rent renegotiation post-relocation? Utilities double? Insurance? Lock down the top 3 suppliers (rent, utilities, insurance) and get quotes. Your relocation was smart; your lease terms may not be. Target:

staff.advice:
• **Redistribute Grace's classes immediately:** Her Build: Lower (17), Build: Upper (9), Elevate (6), and Strongevity (3) sessions need to move to JoJo and Eilis. JoJo should take Build: Lower (strong technical fit); Eilis can absorb Elevate and Strongevity to develop range and confidence away from Tuesdays.

• **Shift Grace's PT load to JoJo:** Grace's PT workload doesn't exist in the data for JoJo, who is already carrying 44 PT sessions. Confirm whether her PT clients are transferring to JoJo or if new member acquisition should pause during handover; if they transfer, JoJo may need PT cap review.

• **Use Eilis's spare capacity for No Sweat Intros:** Grace ran 3 No Sweat Intros last month. Eilis (currently under-loaded at 48 sessions vs JoJo's 133) should own member onboarding, freeing Grace entirely and giving Eilis predictable, lower-pressure delivery.

• **Delegate all Body Composition Scans to admin or rotating duty:** JoJo and Eilis combined logged 31 scans (not real coaching). Move these off coach schedules entirely or rotate as a 15-minute admin task—this reclaims ~5 hours of coach time monthly without losing revenue.

• **Build a Tuesday safety net for JoJo:** JoJo is technically the only strong Tuesday coach. Cross-train Eilis on a non-Tuesday class type (Circuits, Capacity, or Plate) so she can backfill other slots and prevent burnout collapse if JoJo is absent.

• **Cap JoJo's PT and monitor load:** At 133 sessions (44 PT alone), J

class_stats.suggestion:
**For quiet studio time gaps:**

1. **Add a weekly paid PT block (90-min) on Saturday mornings** — your quietest day with only 203 attendees. Offer 3–4 concurrent PT slots at premium pricing to capture trainers and their clients who'd otherwise use the space idle.

2. **Launch a 30-min "Open Gym + Coach" drop-in on Thursday evenings** between classes — Thursday is your second-quietest day (389). Charge £5–8 per session; members get equipment access plus form tips from a roaming coach, filling dead time and building habit of mid-week visits.

**To lift the quietest classes:**

3. **Move Engine and Capacity to Wednesday peak-time slots (your busiest day, 552 attendees).** Quieter classes benefit from foot traffic and social proof; test one quiet class in a Wed 6–7pm window for 4 weeks with a targeted email to members who've attended *any* Build class, positioning it as "next-level conditioning." If it lifts, roll the other there.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Build — Execution Playbook for The Forge

**1. Audit what you're ACTUALLY selling right now**
Stop guessing. For 3 days, track every service/package/addon a member or coach mentions. Document pricing, what's bundled, what's negotiable. You'll find inconsistencies — that's your starting point, not a problem.

**2. Price backwards from your unit economics, not competitors**
Figure out: What monthly revenue do you need per active member to hit your targets? Divide by average classes attended. That's your baseline membership price. Then layer: drop-in rates, class packages, PT add-ons. Make sure the math works *for The Forge*, not industry standards.

**3. Create two separate menus with ONE source of truth**
Build a master spreadsheet (Google Sheets, not Word docs). Coaches see: class capacity, pricing, what they get paid per class. Members see: cost, what's included, duration, skill level. Pull both from the same sheet so you update once, not twice. Share the member version as a clean PDF/image on your site and Instagram.

**4. Test-drive the menu internally first**
Walk 5-7 long-term members and 3 coaches through the new menu separately. Ask: "What's confusing? Would you pay this? What's missing?" Iterate based on real pushback, then launch.

**5. Make it a sales tool, not just a list**
Add clarity on WHY: "Unlimited membership includes 4 coach consultations monthly" (shows value beyond classes). Show transformation outcomes tied to packages. This isn't just a menu—it's your positioning.

**Timeline: 2

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement - Execution Plan for Grace

**1. Run a 2-hour "objection audit" this week**
Pull your last 50 enquiries/cancellations and note exactly what questions people ask before joining. Map these directly onto your landing pages—don't guess. If women repeatedly ask "what if I'm not fit enough?" that needs front-and-centre copy with proof (before/after stories, beginner class availability). This IS your content roadmap.

**2. Create a single "clarity hierarchy" page**
Women's fitness has noise. One landing page should answer in this order: (1) Who this is for—explicitly name your ideal member, (2) What they'll actually DO in a week (real class schedule, not vague "strength training"), (3) What they WON'T get (no intimidation, no men, no unsolicited advice). Remove every buzzword like "empower" or "transformation"—replace with specifics.

**3. A/B test the ONE thing stopping conversions**
Pick the weakest funnel step (sign-up page? pricing clarity?). Identify one specific change (e.g., moving pricing higher, adding "no contract" guarantee, showing instructor bios). Run it for 2 weeks, measure. Don't overhaul everything—precision beats effort here.

**4. Embed FAQ directly into page flow**
Don't hide FAQs at the bottom. If "How much does it cost?" is asked 40% of the time, answer it above the fold. Structure each section as: statement → anticipated question → answer. Your page should feel like it's reading her mind.

**What metric tells you it's working?** Enquiry

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Internal Member Feedback System — Execution for The Forge

**Make it genuinely frictionless — or it dies**
Don't create a "form." Post a laminated A5 card by the water station with 3 questions (What worked? What didn't? One thing we should add?). Members scribble during cool-down. Takes 60 seconds. You read them weekly. This beats a Google Form by miles because it's *there*, it's social, and completion will actually happen.

**Tie feedback directly to visible action**
After month one, pick the single most-requested thing (longer yoga slots, better showers, protein shake partnership) and implement it by month two. Announce: "You asked, we listened." Post a photo of the feedback card that sparked it. This trains members that their voice moves the needle — they'll keep participating.

**Use feedback as your conversion/retention playbook**
Track emerging themes in a simple spreadsheet (3 columns: feedback type | frequency | action taken). New members ask "why should I join?" — you show them *member feedback drove our squat racks upgrade* or *our class schedule changed because members told us what worked.* Turns you into a member-led business, not a gym owner guessing.

**Assign ONE person ownership (probably you initially)**
Monthly 15-min review every Monday morning. Share one insight in your next class briefing. You're building community intelligence and showing members this isn't theater.

**Month 3+: graduate to post-class verbal check-ins during peak hours**
Two minutes catching 3-4 members as they leave. Casual. Personal. Spot-check the themes. This becomes your competitive moat — direct member relationships the

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Strategy for The Forge

**1. Start with your revenue-tier SOPs first**
Write for Personal Training + Fuel Forward before the others — these generate the highest margins and most member friction points. Getting these bulletproof will give you quick wins to build momentum and reveal your documentation style.

**2. Audit what's already in people's heads**
Before writing a single SOP, shadow your best staff member delivering each service for 2-3 hours. Watch where they improvise, repeat themselves, or handle edge cases. This becomes your SOP skeleton — you're documenting excellence that exists, not inventing process theater.

**3. Structure each SOP in 3 layers: client journey, staff execution, failure points**
Don't write procedures in isolation. For each service, create: (a) what the member experiences step-by-step, (b) what staff does at each touchpoint, (c) common problems and how to handle them. This prevents SOPs that look good but don't actually run the business.

**4. Make SOPs scannable and immediately usable**
Use checklists, not paragraphs. New staff should be able to hand a member Fuel Forward SOP and know exactly what they're getting; they should be able to reference Personal Training SOP in 30 seconds when a client asks a specific question. Format for speed, not comprehensiveness.

**5. Assign one person ownership + a 3-week deadline**
Pick your operations person (or you, if you have to). One SOP per week. Share drafts with your team for 48-hour feedback. Lock it down. Move on. Done beats perfect here.

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Calendar Execution for The Forge

• **Batch-create content in 90-minute sprints twice monthly** — block Sundays 2-3:30pm to film 6-8 short clips (transformation stories, quick workouts, member spotlights, behind-the-scenes). Use your phone + ring light. This kills "what do I post today?" paralysis and costs £0.

• **Use a free template (Canva + Google Sheets hybrid)** — create a simple spreadsheet with columns: Date | Platform | Content Type | Caption | Image/Video | Publish Time. Plug in themes: Motivation Monday, Transformation Tuesday, Workout Wednesday, Win Friday. Replicates posts 80% of the way—members won't notice patterns, algorithm rewards consistency.

• **Anchor posts to real gym moments** — don't manufacture content. Every time a member hits a PR, reaches a milestone, or crushes a class, snap a photo with permission. These convert 3x better than generic fitness quotes and take 10 seconds to capture.

• **Schedule everything on Buffer or Meta Business Suite** — 15 minutes per week to schedule next 2 weeks' posts at optimal times (typically 6am, 12pm, 6pm for women 25-45 in Belfast). No manual posting = no missed days, no excuses.

• **Audit what actually drives inquiries** — track which post types drive DM questions or gym visits for 4 weeks. Double down on that format. You might find member stories demolish motivational quotes—so shift 60% of budget there.

**Start tomorrow: Create your content calendar template. Film 8 videos this Sunday.**

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Executing the Videography Contract with Jordan

**Lock in the cadence NOW**
- Book all 6 sessions for the next year in one go (next Tuesday). Non-negotiable recurring slots = Jordan commits capacity to you, you get predictable content pipeline. Negotiate a 10-15% discount for the annual commitment.

**Define what "8 weeks" actually means operationally**
- Pick the same day/time each session (e.g., third Tuesday, 10am-2pm). Build a rolling content calendar showing which gym moments you're capturing next: transformations, class energy, member spotlights, facility upgrades. This prevents vague briefs and ensures Jordan delivers what drives actual sign-ups.

**Make the footage immediately monetizable**
- Before each shoot, identify 2-3 specific conversion angles: new member testimonial, "why women choose The Forge" angle, class showcase. Brief Jordan explicitly on these. You want 5-8 short clips per session ready for Instagram/TikTok/website within 5 days—not a raw 2-hour file you never edit.

**Tie it to revenue metrics**
- Track: which videography outputs drive trial sign-ups and paid memberships. After 3 sessions, analyze what's working. Adjust the brief for sessions 4-6. If testimonial videos convert at 3x the rate of facility tours, you know exactly what to ask Jordan to focus on.

**Build a content runway**
- Stagger releases. Don't dump all footage at once. Plan: 1 clip per week minimum across channels. This keeps The Forge visible in members' feeds without looking desperate or spammy.

What's your current conversion rate

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Systemizing Trial Enquiry Automations – The Forge (6-Week Trial)

• **Audit the current leak** – Pull your last 30 trial enquiries. Track where each one dropped off (didn't book a trial, booked but didn't convert, ghosted after day 1). This tells you exactly which automation touchpoint is broken before you rebuild it.

• **Build a 3-email sequence, not 5** – Day 1 (confirm + logistics + "here's what day 1 looks like for women like you"), Day 3 (social proof + schedule reminder), Day 7 (if no booking: "last 2 spots this week"). Women-specific pain points in subject lines. Test send to yourself first—clunky copy kills conversions.

• **Add a text checkpoint at Day 2** – Email alone won't cut it. A simple SMS ("Hi [name], excited to meet you tomorrow at 6pm?") catches hesitators before they ghost. Use Klaviyo or Twilio integration; set it to trigger only if they haven't booked yet.

• **Tie the automation to your intake form** – Make the form itself part of your system. Ask 1–2 qualifying questions (what's holding you back from fitness right now?) so your follow-up feels personal, not robotic. Automation that's personalized converts 2–3x better.

• **Set a weekly Monday audit for 4 weeks** – Check enquiry-to-trial conversion rate weekly. If it stalls below 60%, tweak email #2. Your 6-week trial is the proof of concept; this data becomes your benchmark for scaling.

**Speed this up:** Do

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Role-Based Weekly Checklists + KPIs for The Forge

• **Start with output, work backwards to tasks.** Don't list activities—list what each person *must deliver* weekly. JoJo (if sales): new member sign-ups + retention calls completed. Eilis (if ops): class fill rates + facility condition score. Sarah (if programming): member satisfaction scores + class innovation implemented. Then reverse-engineer the 3-4 actions that actually drive those KPIs.

• **Make KPIs visible and competitive.** Post a simple shared tracker (even a printed sheet updated Friday) showing each person's weekly numbers. Women respond to transparency and friendly accountability—this creates momentum without micromanagement. Update it together in a 10-min huddle.

• **Lock in the "non-negotiables" first.** For each role, identify 1-2 things that *must* happen weekly or revenue/retention dies (e.g., "member check-in calls" or "class schedule confirmation"). Everything else is secondary. Write these as must-do tasks with a checkbox—no ambiguity.

• **Build in a 15-min Friday review ritual.** Each person reviews their own checklist, flags blockers, and tells you one win. This takes 45 mins total, kills excuses, and gives you early warning on problems. Make it standard so no one dreads it.

• **Document it as a one-page PDF per role** with their name at the top—makes it personal, easy to reference, and signals you're serious. Include: Weekly tasks (checkbox format) | KPIs they own | Friday review slots.

Want help drafting the actual checklists?

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Running Your Vision Session: The Execution Play

• **Structure it as 3 separate 20-min blocks, not one long meeting** — SSO (5 min pitch + 5 min Q&A), 90-day plan (10 min walkthrough of your 3 key priorities), then individual lanes (assign people to breakout rooms in pairs to discuss their specific role). People absorb better in shorter bursts, and the breakouts force real dialogue instead of passive listening.

• **Send the 90-day plan 24 hours early as a one-pager** — not a deck. Your team needs to mentally land on it before the session. Frame it as: "Here's where we're headed. Come ready to tell me what you need to crush your part." This shifts them from receivers to problem-solvers immediately.

• **Define "lane" with actual weekly commitments, not job titles** — instead of saying "marketing owns growth," say "Sarah owns: member referral texts (2x weekly), retention calls to churners (Fri mornings), social content calendar (planned Sundays)." People commit to actions, not abstractions. Ask each person to repeat back their top 3 weekly tasks before they leave.

• **End with: "What's blocking you right now?"** — 10 minutes, no solutions needed yet. Just collect the obstacles. You'll use these to unblock them in the next two weeks. Shows you're serious about removing friction.

• **Schedule 1:1s the same week** — 15 min each, within 3 days of the group session. Reinforce their lane, answer individual concerns, and confirm they're locked in.

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalance for The Forge (30 days)

**Week 1: Map actual hours, not job titles**
- Have each team member (instructors, front desk, management) log their daily tasks in 15-min blocks for 5 days—not estimates, *actual time*. You'll spot where people are context-switching between classes, admin, cleaning, member queries. This reveals the real bottlenecks, not assumptions.

**Week 2: Calculate your cost-per-hour drain**
- Total payroll per person ÷ billable/core hours = what over-stretch actually costs you. If your best instructor is 60% admin work, that's revenue-generating capacity you're bleeding. Quantify it—makes rebalancing decisions obvious.

**Week 3: Rebalance using the "one-thing shift" method**
- Don't reshuffle everything. Find ONE task per stretched person that someone else can absorb (e.g., social media posting to front desk during quiet hours, class setup to a part-time cleaner). Test it. One small win beats a chaotic overhaul.

**Ongoing: Automate before you hire**
- Before adding headcount, use Mindbody/Zen Planner to auto-send booking reminders, cancellations to class waitlists, or payment chasing. Kills 3-5 admin hours/week with zero new salary cost.

**By day 30, you should see:** Clearer role boundaries, 1-2 freed-up hours per person weekly, and data to decide if you need hire or just restructure.

# How to Cut 10% from The Forge's Costs — Right Now

**Before you start:** Audit your last 3 months of P&L. Find your three biggest expense buckets (likely: staff wages, rent/utilities, equipment/supplies). You're hunting for quick wins first, structural cuts second.

• **Staff scheduling → immediate savings**: Map peak vs. quiet hours. Cut one session per week in your slowest slot (probably midweek mornings). Reduce that shift to part-time cover or cross-train existing staff. This alone typically saves 4-6% without cutting quality hours. Do this this week.

• **Utilities/facility costs**: Get a commercial energy audit quote (often free). Negotiate with your provider NOW—say you're getting competing quotes. Install motion-sensor lighting in changing rooms/storage. If you're on a gym equipment maintenance contract, bundle providers or negotiate annually (don't renew monthly). 2-3% here.

• **Supplier/vendor renegotiation**: Call your cleaning supplier, towel service, and equipment vendor. Tell them you're consolidating to one provider and want a 12-15% discount or you're switching. You'll likely get 8-12% off one of them just by asking. Do 3 calls today. Target 1-2%.

• **Member experience trade-off check**: Before cutting anything, ask: does this touch the member experience? If yes, it costs you more in churn than you save. Stick to back-office and operational efficiency only.

**The 10% target is achievable in 60 days without gutting your gym.** Start with scheduling + negotiation today.

# Monthly Finance KPI Update — Action Plan for Grace

• **Automate the pull, not the manual grind** — Set up a simple Google Sheet or Excel that auto-pulls from your bank feeds (Xero/QuickBooks integration) and booking system. Spend 30 mins setting it up once, then spend 10 mins monthly refreshing. Track: MRR (member subscriptions), churn rate (cancellations), cost-per-acquisition, cash runway.

• **Create a one-page "traffic light" dashboard** — Red/amber/green flags only. Is member count up or down vs. last month? Is cash positive? Are classes hitting capacity targets? Grace needs to glance at one sheet and know instantly what needs her attention—not wade through spreadsheets.

• **Schedule it for the 2nd working day of each month at 9am** — Non-negotiable. Make it a 20-minute ritual the morning after you close the books. Do it before fires start. This prevents the "I'll do it later" death spiral where financials go stale.

• **Identify your ONE leading indicator to watch obsessively** — Probably member retention + class attendance rates (they predict revenue 30 days ahead). Don't track 15 KPIs. Track 3, obsess over 1. That's where your growth lever is.

• **Share results with accountability partner** — Text a business mate or mentor your 3 KPIs by Friday each month. Public commitment forces the update to actually happen.

# Financial Forecasting for The Forge — Execution

• **Pick your ONE critical metric first.** Don't build a 50-line spreadsheet. Start with monthly membership revenue (current base × churn rate) + class pack sales + PT revenue. Model these three separately for 12 months. Everything else is noise until you've nailed cashflow.

• **Use your actual member data, not assumptions.** Pull last 6 months of revenue by category from your POS. Calculate *your* churn rate (how many members quit monthly), not an industry average. This number drives everything—forecast backwards from it.

• **Build a "worst case" column alongside your base case.** What happens if churn jumps to 8% instead of 5%? If you lose a big corporate partnership? Grace needs to see the breaking point and plan contingencies *now*, not when it happens.

• **Set forecast triggers, not just predictions.** Define: "If revenue drops below £X in any month, I'll cut [specific cost]" or "If we don't hit Y new members by March, I'll pivot acquisition spend to Z." Make the forecast *actionable*, not decorative.

• **Do this in Excel/Sheets, not accounting software yet.** You need visibility and speed to adjust. Export bank + POS data, spend 3 hours building it this week, then review monthly. Perfectionism kills momentum.

**What's the current monthly member churn rate you're seeing?** That's your starting number.

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Profitability Mapping for The Forge

**1. Start with revenue buckets TODAY**
Pull last 3 months of actual data and split it: class memberships (recurring), PT sessions (hourly rate × bookings), add-ons (retail, smoothies, challenges). Don't overthink categories—if it's under 5% of revenue, lump it. You need this done this week, not perfect in a month.

**2. Assign costs ruthlessly by service**
- **Classes**: Split studio rent/utilities by class hours, instructor pay (hourly), music/app subscriptions
- **PT**: Direct instructor commission + small % of overhead (you're already paying rent regardless)
- **Add-ons**: COGS only—don't allocate your time yet

*Reality check: You'll find 1-2 services are subsidizing others. That's the insight.*

**3. Use a one-page Google Sheet, not accounting software**
Columns: Revenue | Direct Costs | Contribution Margin | % Margin. One row per service. Include "blended" membership revenue as one line (don't split it 10 ways—you can't measure anyway). Calculate margin, not profit.

**4. Test the numbers with your PT staff and class instructors**
Ask them: "Does this cost feel right?" You'll catch your own blind spots and they'll tell you what's actually killing profitability (usually: off-peak class attendance, unpaid admin time, or underpriced PT).

**5. Act on ONE finding within 2 weeks**
Margin too thin on add-ons? Bundle them into membership tiers. PT underperforming? Raise rates 10

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Audit & Fix Automations — The Forge

**Immediate action (today):**
- Pull your full automation audit log from whatever tool you're using (Zapier/Make/native CRM). Screenshot every single active automation with its trigger and action. You'll spot duplicates instantly—look for workflows firing on the same trigger (e.g., "new form submission") with overlapping outputs. Kill the redundant ones now.

**Enquiry-to-trial flow stress test (this week):**
- Create 3 fake contact submissions yourself (phone, email, form) using different entry points. Track what happens: Does she get the welcome email? The trial booking link? A follow-up? When? If any step is missing or delayed >2 hours, that's leakage you're losing money on. Fix the broken automations first before scaling ads.

**Common Forge-specific blocker:**
- Women-only gyms often have the trial booking step broken—either the calendar link doesn't sync to your class schedule, or automated reminders hit at the wrong time. Verify: Does the automation pull real, available trial slots? Does it send the reminder 24hrs before (not 48hrs)?

**Remove this friction point:**
- If you're using multiple tools (form tool + CRM + email), automations will duplicate. Consolidate: All enquiries → single CRM record → single automation sequence. One source of truth.

**Accountability check:**
- Map the entire flow on paper. Where do you manually touch it? Those are your bottlenecks. Automate those manual steps or eliminate them.

What tool are you using for automations?

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge: Concrete Action Plan

**Task 1: Member Onboarding Emails**
- Use Zapier + ChatGPT to auto-generate personalized welcome sequences triggered by new sign-ups (fitness goals, experience level pulled from intake form)
- This saves 2-3 hours/week and increases retention because members get immediate engagement instead of silence
- Setup cost: 30 mins; ROI: huge (one retained member = £500+ annual value)

**Task 2: Social Media Content Calendar Population**
- Feed ChatGPT your past top-performing posts + monthly themes → it generates 15-20 caption/hashtag combos weekly
- You + team just pick/tweak/schedule in Buffer instead of brainstorming from scratch
- Reality check: This frees 4-5 hours/week for actual member interaction, which drives word-of-mouth better than content quantity

**Task 3: FAQ/Inquiry Response Triage**
- Deploy a simple AI chatbot (Tidio or ManyChat on WhatsApp) to handle the repetitive "What are your hours?", "Do you have childcare?", "Can I do a free trial?" questions
- Collects info, routes genuine inquiries to you, handles 60-70% of incoming noise automatically
- Critical: This removes admin friction so you're only handling qualified interest

**Speed execution:** Pick task #2 first (social). It's fastest to set up (1 hour) and you'll see weekly time savings immediately. Builds confidence for the harder integrations.

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity Execution for The Forge Website

• **Don't build from scratch — use WordPress plugins TODAY.** Install Wordfence (free tier) + Sucuri WAF within the next 2 hours. This blocks 90% of attacks without hiring anyone. If you're on Shopify/Squarespace, their built-in security is sufficient — skip this step.

• **Audit payment/member data NOW.** If you're collecting card details or storing passwords: STOP. Use Stripe or Square for payments (PCI-compliant by default) and force password resets for all members this week. One breach tanks your reputation permanently in Belfast.

• **Get an SSL certificate live immediately.** Check your hosting provider's dashboard — it's usually one-click free. Your booking/login pages MUST show the padlock icon. Members won't enter payment info otherwise.

• **Create a password policy you can actually enforce.** Don't overcomplicate it — require 12+ characters for admin accounts only. Train yourself and any staff on *not* using "ForgeGym123" as the default. Takes 15 minutes max.

• **Book a 1-hour security audit with a local tech freelancer (£150-250 on Upwork).** They'll stress-test your login, find what you missed, and give you a priority fix list. Cheaper than a data breach fine. Do this within 2 weeks, not 2 months.

**The real win:** Focus on payment security first. Everything else is secondary to protecting member trust.

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

Your cost-per-lead has exploded 4.5× in four months (£6 → £27) whilst lead volume has collapsed 94% (69 → 4). This isn't budget fatigue—you've *cut* spend to £109, which suggests either the account is paused/throttled, the campaign structure broke, or you've stopped optimising entirely. The 75% close rate in July is healthy, but you're generating almost no pipeline to convert. April's £6 CPL at £435 spend is your true benchmark; you're nowhere near it now.

The trajectory reads like creative burnout + audience saturation compounded by under-spending. At £109/month you're below Meta's learning threshold; the algorithm can't optimise, and you're fighting auction noise. July feels like the ads ran for 1–2 weeks, not a full month.

**Actions**

1. **Reactivate the account properly.** Pull the full July campaign report—check if ads were paused, had delivery issues, or ran only partial days. If the budget was genuinely live, you've hit severe creative fatigue. Audit which creative (video/carousel/static, copy angle) drove April's 69 leads. Rebuild from that template: test 3–4 fresh variations (different hook, different pain point—e.g. "no judgement," "strength training for women," "post-baby fitness") and refresh every 2 weeks.

2. **Reset budget to £350–400/month and layered targeting.** You need scale to feed the algorithm and hit statistical significance. Split into: (a) Broad lookalike (1% LLA from April converters), (b) Interest-based (women 25–55, fitness + wellness + health), (c) Saved audience (website visitors + email list if you have it). Run each at £100–130. This replicates your April structure.

3. **Test a different offer angle or lead magnet.** "6-week trial" may have saturated your local Belfast audience. Test: free consultation call, downloadable "Beginner's Strength Guide," or "First session free + nutrition plan." Track which offer holds the best close rate. July's 75
